item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Anyone else thinking that the "auditory hallucinations" are its attempts to communicate? Not sure if that was the intended implication or just my interpretation. I feel like the ringing sound beginning during the surgery probably denotes that they were causing him pain, which makes me…sad.

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: This image is a photograph by Duane Michals, titled The Illuminated Man, 1968. As Mssr. Michals has not released this under Creative Commons 3.0, this is a violation of his copyright and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How many scientists does it take to screw in an SCP-1093?

Very well written, and interesting (even more so with the surgical procedure included).
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: moth be like l a m p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reminded me a little of MC&D, sounds like something they would sell, which is good.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, incomprehensible yet not random, it gives the impression of definite intent yet one has no ideas as to what that intent actually is. That said it does feel slightly random, in that the effects seem slightly unrelated to each other which takes away from it's sense of depth. Anyway +1

item.title: Re: Questions, observations and nitpicks.
item.content:encoded: Wow. Thorough. I second all that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this article was MADE for me (cuz of my screenname

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The surgical paradox was cool, and almost got my up-vote, but I gotta point out that "radiation" and "light" are often interchangeable. If it isn't lit up in it's active state but still producing radiation, that would just mean that the wavelength isn't in the visible spectrum. Perhaps you mean the SCP's body is radioactive, like it's constantly putting off β particles or something.

item.title: Questions, observations and nitpicks.
item.content:encoded:
1) Why such a big storage area? It appears to be functionally inert, totally immobile, it doesn't exchange gas/fluid with it's environment (1a, does it sweat? I'm assuming no) and needs to be stored behind a foot of lead shielding, why not just stick it in a 3'x5'x8' lead sarcophagus. A 5mx5mx(no vertical dimension?) room lined with the same shielding seems excessive, possibly wasteful of resources.

2) "Is" vs "Appears to be". I'd argue that it is demonstrably NOT a Caucasian male anything based on the traits that make it containment-worthy. At best it's wearing (most of) the skin of a Caucasian male human.

3) What type of light? What wavelengths are present?

4) Is there a head-shape made of light that gives off this light, or does the light emanate from a central point located approximately where the middle of the head would presumably be were there a head?

5) What kind of radiation?

6) Is there a cycle or pattern to the variations in radiation intensity? Does it vary depending on where you place the measuring instrument? Is it blocked by the body? Does the body seem to be affected by it?

7) Nitpicking here. A household light switch with on-off positions is not itself activated, it activates or deactivates the light-emitting function depending on which position it's in. The switch itself is either in the "on" or "off" position.

8) Nitpicking again. It doesn't display steady, constant breathing if it doesn't detectably take in or expel air, it displays chest/torso movement consistent with steady, constant breathing.

9) I don't think it would be specifically noted that it's not capable of speech "…in this state…" since it's not capable of speech in the other state and appears to lack any of the necessary apparatus in both states.

10) What happens in the rare cases when the eventual sudden desistance of auditory hallucinations never happens?

What's here is interesting, but it feels like 70% of an SCP write-up rather than the complete document. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is only pretending to look human!!

Also, I came here because of SCP-1472.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hadn't seen this before SCP-1472. Having now read it, my only issues are a) it needs a third dimension in its containment cell and b) is there a reason it's being photographed in a dank tunnel? Upvoted regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so fucking weird. I really like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've clarified and fixed all of these issues, as requested.

Due to the recurring criticism over the use of the word "autopsy" I've changed it to a "Surgical Report"/"Surgical Examination"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The word "autopsy" really sticks out for me. Autopsies are for dead things, specifically dead humans. But I'm not sure what a better word for what happens here would be, "biopsy" feels more like sample-taking and "dissection" means actual dissasembly…"surgical examination" maybe? Also, what was the bodily reaction after the surgery? Big flaps of skin were peeled away (and presumably laid back), that should leave some long-term effects. A note on wether it healed normally over the course of months with the expected scarring, or faster than normal with no scarring, or instantly like self-sealing rubber seems called for.

Why is respiration deemed odd? Presumably the neck has a trachea hole in it, he'd just breath through that, not unlike a stoma.

The detailed description of clothing seems unnecessary. It would be one thing if the clothes had some special properties, but it doesn't sound like they do. If they stripped him nakes, would anything change? If not, then the clothes don't need mentioning.

Last nitpick, "…ranging from 30 to 35 years of age." makes it sound like the age is fluctuating. On Monday he'll be 34 and on Tuesday he may be 32, sort of thing. How about "…between 30 and 35 years old." instead?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wasn't intending to imply that there was ever more than one. I'll rephrase that to clarify any confusion.

I can see where you're coming from when it comes to calling it an autopsy. Though, one could argue that SCP-1093 isn't technically alive when it's deactivated. If it's enough of an issue, I'm fine with renaming it to a "Surgical Report" or something like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this needs a lot of proofreading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm curious… It may just be a wording issue, but the description implies that there's exactly one of this thing. But then in the autopsy report, you describe "a (de)activated 1093 specimen". Are/were there multiples of this thing, and there's only one left? If not I think it should be written as "As of the writing of this report, no surgery on SCP-1093 has been performed during its active state".

Also: autopsy is usually reserved for something that's dead and being taken completely apart. Would you mean exploratory surgery or like that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He stands upright when activated, but his upper body goes limp when he's deactivated. Imagine a person with their legs straightened and hanging their torso limply in front of them facing the floor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay. It just seemed odd that it was specified his upper body went limp when in fact, he was never moving at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He's completely stationary all the time. He's quite literally a "Lamp Man" as his only feasible function is to be a human being that operates like a lamp, but hints of there being something more to him are left in several of his unexplained features.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good point, Brojo Jojo.

Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm almost surprised this isn't a Little Mister, though I don't think there's a space on the list for him. The autopsy report was really intriguing and I +1'd for that.

One question: he's inert when switched on, and his upper body (just the upper body?) goes limp when he's switched off. So does he ever do anything on his own?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The description should explicitly note that the on/off state doesn't affect the radiation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It wears a standard white button-up dress shirt, black kaki pants, and brown Oxford dress shoes.

All the time? Did the Foundation's wardrobe budget get cut this quarter? Do we not have a bathrobe we can give him on laundry day?

Joking aside, what i'm trying to say is that, if the SCP is a physical entity in our custody, describing what it wears makes it sound like it's a cartoon character that only has one outfit because that's all the animators can be bothered to draw. If it's an ethereal being, or something the Foundation hasn't contained yet, or its clothes are part of its body, then describing its wardrobe makes sense. In this case, it's just fluff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yet no one mentions it, peculiar to say the least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably because this is something you learn in high school science class, and isn't something that needs to be mentioned in a site dominated by college-level authors.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is this not mentioned in the guide? several of the older articles also feature enumerated measurements. Not to mention that no one mentions this (aside from yourself, from what I've seen).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Measurements should not be enumerated; it's "168 cm", not "one hundred sixty eight (168) cm". Also, I'm pretty sure that in SI it would be 1.68 meters anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Despite its flaws, I enjoyed it. +1. Good work sassy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What would you suggest I cut from the article? Any information provided that strikes you as particularly pointless?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree about adding more creepiness than danger. I removed the ending section solely for that reason, being skeptical of adding it to begin with. I may add more concerning the auditory hallucinations, and will reclassify it as Safe. It was only classified as Euclid due to the late Document 7031H-1093, anyways.

Thanks for the feedback

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
too much information for something that does very little and would be effectively contained by sticking it in a lead closet with the switch mechanically held in the "off" position

not that this is a terrible object, just your article could be half this length and still be more than enough information.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Doctor's decent into the clutches of the SCP can still be saved, I think, but would require some serious re-write to not break the sense of belief. Otherwise, this has been upvoted. Good read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, I had to downvote, because I'm just … not digging it.

There seem to be two concepts at work which are entirely separate from each other … the form that the SCP takes and the effect it has, with no obvious connection between the two. Frankly I found the idea of a guy with a light-bulb for a head and a lightswitch on his back a bit too comical, and the hallucinations that it causes a bit too vague to be scary. It causes hallucinations which make people hear ringing and drilling, which seem to drive people nuts. I think it needs more creep-factor when it comes to that.

I'd suggest re-writing it as a Safe rather than an Euclid, and playing up the more sinister creep rather than the "it is dangerous and it can most definitely kill you" stuff. That part feels a little forced. Good potential SCP though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I was definitely on the fence about adding that part. I felt like the SCP-1093 was a little too mundane, but I do agree that I may have gone considerably over-the-top on that section. I feel like I should remove it entirely.

EDIT: And so I did. Whether or not this benefits the article is up to you. I think it does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fairly good for most of it, except for the events involving the doctor. Suspension of disbelief was broken when he barricaded himself in a room for six days, in which time no security team came to deal with the obvious screaming, and then was apparently allowed to just walk into an SCP containment unit.

That entire section feels tacked-on to make the SCP dangerous. It doesn't need to be more dangerous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure if I like or dislike this article, withholding vote for now.
